one_line_takeaway: Averaging memorization over the forget set hides extractable outliers; judging and controlling unlearning per-sequence (via the outlier-count metric plus SGA/TAU) yields both stronger privacy and better utility, with method choice depending on model and forget-set size.
key_finding: No single method dominates across the (model-size x forget-set-size) space, but the per-sequence perspective wins. SGA gives the strongest privacy everywhere (at most ~1.8 extractable outliers) and, for large models (>1B params), also best preserves text-generation utility. For small models (125M) plain gradient-based methods are destructive -- GA/SGA lose ~80% of dialogue F1 -- whereas the task-arithmetic methods (TA, TAU) lose only ~30%, making TA/TAU the right choice for small models and small forget sets. TAU is reported to give up to a 3X text-generation improvement over the previous SOTA (GA). A recurring result: after unlearning, reasoning/classification accuracy degrades far less than generative (dialogue) ability across all model sizes.
core_contribution: The paper argues that unlearning of memorized training text in LLMs must be judged and controlled at the level of the INDIVIDUAL textual sequence, not by the average memorization score over the whole forget set. Its own novel contributions are: (1) a new success metric that counts memorization-score OUTLIERS in the forget set after unlearning (c(Df), Eq. 3), rather than only checking that the mean memorization falls below a threshold (Eq. 2, the prior success criterion from Jang et al. 2022); (2) a new Membership Inference Attack (MIA) demonstrating that aggregate-only SOTA unlearning (Gradient Ascent) leaves under-/over-memorized subpopulations that remain extractable; (3) a model-indistinguishability metric (loss-based closeness between the unlearned model and a from-scratch retrained model); and (4) two new unlearning algorithms, Selective Gradient Ascent (SGA) and Task Arithmetic for Unlearning (TAU). Note that plain Gradient Ascent (GA, Jang et al. 2022) and plain Task Arithmetic (TA, Ilharco et al. 2022, originally for detoxification) are prior/cited methods used as baselines; the paper's insight is that TA -- never before used for LLM unlearning -- can itself be SOTA for small models, and it wraps its per-sequence perspective around GA (=>SGA) and TA (=>TAU).
threat_model: White-box adversary with access to model weights attempting to extract memorized/verbatim training sequences (PII, copyrighted text) from a pretrained LLM, in the context of Right-To-Be-Forgotten requests. The paper stresses two privacy loopholes that aggregate-average unlearning ignores: highly-memorized outliers remain trivially extractable, AND sequences unlearned 'too well' (very low memorization) become extractable via a Streisand-effect / MIA angle. The authors instantiate this with their own new MIA against GA-unlearned models. Privacy is operationalized as the model's propensity to regurgitate training text verbatim (exact memorization), not as protection of higher-level entity/factual associations, which they explicitly place out of scope.
limitations: The authors state there is no single best method across the whole problem space (a known issue in the unlearning field). The outlier band bounds (lb=15%, ub=60%) and the memorization limit gamma are set empirically and are dataset-dependent, so the privacy metric's calibration is not principled. Scope is restricted to exact verbatim memorization of individual sequences; unlearning higher-level entity/factual associations (e.g. auditing that no residual links between an individual and their PII survive) is explicitly out of scope and flagged as needing a further auditing process. The full MIA and indistinguishability results, hardware, and hyperparameters live in appendices (G/H/C) not included in the reviewed pages. SGA/TAU incur higher runtime than GA/TA (e.g. TAU 50.74 min vs GA 7.22 min on 125M in Table 2), though the authors argue this is negligible versus retraining.

datasets_used: The Pile (pretraining corpus for GPT-Neo; forget sequences of 200 tokens sampled from it)
Training Data Extraction Challenge samples (Jang 2022) -- easy-to-extract Pile sequences used as forget sets
Wizard of Wikipedia (WoW) -- dialogue generation utility
Wizard of Internet (WoI) -- dialogue generation utility
Blended Skill Talk (BST) -- dialogue generation utility
quantitative_results: GPT-Neo forgetting thresholds (avg memorization over 10,000 unseen Pile sequences): 29.94% (125M), 33.27% (1.3B), 34.02% (2.7B)
EXTRACTABLE outliers (band 15%-60%), 16-sample forget set: SGA = 1.8 (125M) / 1.0 (1.3B) / 0.8 (2.7B); GA = 5.0 / 4.6 / 7.0; DEFAULT (no unlearning) = 13.8 / 16.0 / 16.0
Dialogue F1 (125M, 16 samples): DEFAULT 10.27, GA 2.28, SGA 1.86, TA 7.18, TAU 7.37 -- gradient methods lose ~80% F1 while TA/TAU lose ~30%
Dialogue F1 (1.3B, 16 samples): DEFAULT 12.86, GA 11.34, SGA 12.63, TAU 12.52, TA 9.60
Cross-entropy (2.7B, 16 samples): DEFAULT 3.09, GA 3.27, SGA 3.32, TAU 3.27 (TAU on 2.7B reported to match/exceed original model's generation)
Classification accuracy (2.7B, 16 samples): DEFAULT 56.94, SGA 57.22, TAU 55.14, GA 56.25 -- reasoning far more robust than generation
Runtime minutes (125M): GA 7.22, SGA 8.18, TA 7.91, TAU 50.74; (1.3B): GA 16.23, SGA 20.17, TAU 29.96
SGA gives at most ~1.8 extractable samples across all experiments; TAU reported ~3X text-generation improvement over prior SOTA (GA)
baselines_compared: Gradient Ascent (GA) -- Jang et al. 2022, prior SOTA for memorized-data unlearning
Task Arithmetic (TA) -- Ilharco et al. 2022, originally a detoxification method, adapted here as a baseline for unlearning
Differential Privacy Decoding (DPD) -- Majmudar et al. 2022, an inference-time (weight-preserving) method used to expose utility-privacy trade-offs
DEFAULT (original model before any unlearning) as reference
